content.text: Blameless Postmortem Template and Timeline

A blameless postmortem is a structured retrospective that examines what happened during an incident without attributing fault to individuals. The assumption is that engineers act reasonably given the information, tools, and constraints available at the time. The postmortem process produces two outputs: a written document that captures the full incident narrative, and a set of prioritized action items that reduce the likelihood or severity of similar incidents in the future.

For agentic AI systems, postmortems carry additional weight because failures often involve emergent behavior, multi-hop tool chains, or model outputs that are difficult to reproduce deterministically. A well-structured postmortem template ensures that teams capture not just the system state at failure time, but also the context windows, tool call sequences, and evaluation signals that shaped the agent's behavior.

attrs.alt: Blameless postmortem incident timeline and document section map

content.text: Postmortem Initiation Criteria and Timing

Not every incident warrants a full postmortem, but the threshold should be set lower for agentic AI systems than for conventional software. A postmortem should be triggered for: any SEV-1 or SEV-2 incident, any incident that caused customer data exposure or safety boundary violation, any incident where the agent took an irreversible action not intended by the user, and any incident that required manual intervention to halt agent execution.

The postmortem document should be opened within 24 hours of resolution. The initial draft is populated by the Incident Commander from the incident timeline, Slack logs, and alert history. A live review meeting should be scheduled within 72 hours while memories are fresh. Delaying beyond 72 hours significantly degrades the quality of context reconstruction, especially for incidents involving non-deterministic model behavior.
Canonical Postmortem Document Template

The following Markdown template is the standard used for all production incidents. Teams should store postmortems in a shared knowledge base (Confluence, Notion, or a Git-tracked docs folder) with a consistent naming scheme: YYYY-MM-DD-INC-{number}-{short-slug}.md.

# Postmortem: [INC-XXXX] [Short Title]

**Status:** Draft / In Review / Final
**Severity:** SEV-1 / SEV-2 / SEV-3
**Incident Commander:** @name
**Lead Engineer:** @name
**Date of Incident:** YYYY-MM-DD
**Duration:** Xh Ym (detection to resolution)
**Date of Postmortem Meeting:** YYYY-MM-DD

---

## Impact Summary

| Dimension          | Value                              |
|--------------------|------------------------------------|
| Users Affected     | ~N (% of active users)             |
| Revenue Impact     | $X (estimated)                     |
| SLO Budget Burned  | X% of monthly error budget         |
| External Customers | Yes / No (N enterprise accounts)   |
| Agent Tool Calls   | N total; M irreversible            |

---

## Timeline of Events (UTC)

| Time (UTC)   | Event                                           | Who Noticed   |
|--------------|-------------------------------------------------|---------------|
| HH:MM        | First anomaly observable in traces              | Automated     |
| HH:MM        | Alert fires - on-call paged                     | PagerDuty     |
| HH:MM        | IC declares SEV-X, war room opened              | @ic           |
| HH:MM        | Mitigation applied (rollback / flag off)        | @eng          |
| HH:MM        | Service restored, error rate < threshold        | @ic           |
| HH:MM        | Incident resolved, war room closed              | @ic           |

*For agentic incidents, annotate tool calls and model responses inline.*

---

## Root Cause

*One paragraph. State what the proximate cause was - not who caused it, but what
condition or change triggered the failure. E.g., "A prompt change deployed at
14:23 UTC removed the stop condition check, causing the agent to call the
delete_file tool recursively until storage quota was exhausted."*

---

## Contributing Factors (Five Whys or Fishbone)

1. **Why did the agent call the tool repeatedly?** -> Stop condition was absent from prompt.
2. **Why was stop condition absent?** -> Prompt diff review did not surface the removal.
3. **Why was it not surfaced?** -> Eval suite did not have a test for recursive tool calls.
4. **Why no eval for recursion?** -> No prior incident surfaced this failure mode.
5. **Why no preventive guardrail?** -> Tool call budget enforcement was not implemented.

---

## What Went Well

- On-call engineer responded within 3 minutes of alert.
- Rollback procedure executed correctly in under 5 minutes.
- Customers notified via status page within 20 minutes.

---

## What Went Wrong

- Prompt diff review did not catch the regression.
- Eval suite lacked coverage for tool call budget exhaustion.
- Agent had no circuit-breaker to self-limit tool call depth.

---

## Where We Got Lucky

- Incident occurred at 02:00 UTC when traffic was 15% of peak.
- Storage quota limit naturally bounded the blast radius.

---

## Action Items

| ID    | Action                                           | Owner     | Due Date   | Priority |
|-------|--------------------------------------------------|-----------|------------|----------|
| AI-01 | Add tool-call budget guardrail to agent runtime  | @eng      | +1 week    | P0       |
| AI-02 | Add recursive tool call eval test to CI          | @qa       | +1 week    | P0       |
| AI-03 | Update prompt review checklist for stop conds    | @pm       | +2 weeks   | P1       |
| AI-04 | Add storage quota alert at 80% threshold         | @sre      | +2 weeks   | P1       |

---

## Lessons Learned

*1-2 sentences summarizing the systemic learning, not just the fix.*

---

## Appendix

- Link to incident Slack thread
- Link to traces / logs
- Link to agent execution replay
- Prompt diff at time of incident
AI-Specific Postmortem Annotations

Standard software postmortems focus on code changes and infrastructure state. Agentic AI postmortems require additional artifacts that capture the model's reasoning context at the time of failure. Without these, root cause analysis is incomplete because the same code can produce different behaviors depending on conversation history, tool call results, and model version.

The appendix of every postmortem for an agentic incident should include: the full system prompt active during the incident (or a diff if it changed), the tool call sequence with inputs and outputs, the model version and temperature settings, any eval scores computed on the incident trace, and a replay link if your platform supports deterministic replay of traces. Storing these artifacts alongside the postmortem enables future teams to understand failure modes that would otherwise be impossible to reconstruct.

# postmortem_artifacts.py - collect and package agent execution artifacts for postmortem
import json
import pathlib
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from datetime import datetime
from typing import List, Optional


@dataclass
class ToolCallRecord:
    call_id: str
    tool_name: str
    input_args: dict
    output: str
    duration_ms: int
    error: Optional[str] = None


@dataclass
class AgentExecutionArtifact:
    incident_id: str
    model_id: str
    model_version: str
    temperature: float
    system_prompt_hash: str
    system_prompt_snapshot: str
    conversation_id: str
    tool_calls: List[ToolCallRecord] = field(default_factory=list)
    eval_scores: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    replay_url: Optional[str] = None
    captured_at: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z")


def capture_artifact(
    incident_id: str,
    agent_session,   # platform-specific session object
    eval_runner,
) -> AgentExecutionArtifact:
    import hashlib

    prompt = agent_session.system_prompt
    prompt_hash = hashlib.sha256(prompt.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]

    tool_calls = [
        ToolCallRecord(
            call_id=tc.id,
            tool_name=tc.name,
            input_args=tc.input,
            output=str(tc.output)[:2000],  # truncate large outputs
            duration_ms=tc.duration_ms,
            error=tc.error,
        )
        for tc in agent_session.tool_calls
    ]

    eval_scores = {}
    try:
        eval_scores = eval_runner.score_trace(agent_session.trace)
    except Exception as e:
        eval_scores = {"error": str(e)}

    return AgentExecutionArtifact(
        incident_id=incident_id,
        model_id=agent_session.model_id,
        model_version=agent_session.model_version,
        temperature=agent_session.temperature,
        system_prompt_hash=prompt_hash,
        system_prompt_snapshot=prompt,
        conversation_id=agent_session.conversation_id,
        tool_calls=tool_calls,
        eval_scores=eval_scores,
        replay_url=agent_session.replay_url,
    )


def save_artifact(artifact: AgentExecutionArtifact, output_dir: str) -> pathlib.Path:
    out = pathlib.Path(output_dir) / f"{artifact.incident_id}-agent-artifact.json"
    out.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    out.write_text(json.dumps(asdict(artifact), indent=2))
    print(f"Artifact saved: {out}")
    return out


if __name__ == "__main__":
    artifact = AgentExecutionArtifact(
        incident_id="INC-2026-0042",
        model_id="claude-3-7-sonnet",
        model_version="20250219",
        temperature=0.0,
        system_prompt_hash="a1b2c3d4e5f6",
        system_prompt_snapshot="You are a file management assistant...",
        conversation_id="conv_abc123",
        tool_calls=[
            ToolCallRecord("tc_1", "list_files", {"path": "/data"}, "[...200 files]", 45),
            ToolCallRecord("tc_2", "delete_file", {"path": "/data/old.csv"}, "deleted", 120),
        ],
        eval_scores={"safety_boundary_respected": 0.0, "task_completion": 1.0},
        replay_url="https://traces.internal/replay/conv_abc123",
    )
    save_artifact(artifact, "/tmp/postmortems/INC-2026-0042")
The Blameless Principle in Practice

Blamelessness is not the same as accountability-free. The blameless postmortem distinguishes between individual blame (which is counterproductive) and systemic accountability (which is required). The question is never 'who wrote the bad prompt?' but instead 'what process allowed a prompt change that removed a critical stop condition to pass review and reach production?'

Facilitators must actively redirect blame during the postmortem meeting. Phrases that should be avoided include: 'who approved this change?', 'why didn't they catch it?', and 'this was obviously a mistake.' Phrases that keep the discussion systemic include: 'what conditions made this outcome possible?', 'what would the system have needed to prevent this?', and 'what would a reasonable engineer have done given the information available?'

For agentic AI incidents, blamelessness must extend to the model itself. The model cannot be blamed for following its instructions — the question is always whether the instructions, guardrails, and monitoring were sufficient to constrain the model's behavior to safe boundaries. This mindset shift is non-obvious and often requires explicit facilitation guidance for teams new to AI system operations.
Automating Postmortem Scaffolding

Manually populating the timeline section of a postmortem is time-consuming and error-prone. Most SRE platforms can auto-generate the initial timeline from alert history, deployment events, and Slack message timestamps. The IC should review and enrich the auto-generated timeline rather than starting from scratch.

# scaffold_postmortem.py - generate postmortem doc skeleton from incident events
import json
from datetime import datetime
from typing import List, Tuple

IncidentEvent = Tuple[str, str, str]  # (timestamp_iso, description, actor)


def generate_timeline_table(events: List[IncidentEvent]) -> str:
    header = "| Time (UTC) | Event                                        | Actor       |"
    sep    = "|------------|----------------------------------------------|-------------|"
    rows = []
    for ts, desc, actor in events:
        dt = datetime.fromisoformat(ts.replace("Z", "+00:00"))
        time_str = dt.strftime("%H:%M")
        rows.append(f"| {time_str}       | {desc:<44} | {actor:<11} |")
    return "\n".join([header, sep] + rows)


def generate_impact_table(
    users_affected: int,
    error_budget_pct: float,
    revenue_impact_usd: float,
    external_customers: int,
    tool_calls: int,
    irreversible_calls: int,
) -> str:
    rev = f"${revenue_impact_usd:,.0f} (estimated)"
    return (
        "| Dimension          | Value                              |\n"
        "|--------------------|-----------------------------------|\n"
        f"| Users Affected     | ~{users_affected:,}                        |\n"
        f"| Revenue Impact     | {rev:<34} |\n"
        f"| SLO Budget Burned  | {error_budget_pct:.1f}% of monthly error budget     |\n"
        f"| External Customers | {external_customers} enterprise accounts            |\n"
        f"| Agent Tool Calls   | {tool_calls} total; {irreversible_calls} irreversible        |"
    )


def scaffold_postmortem(
    incident_id: str,
    title: str,
    severity: str,
    ic: str,
    lead_eng: str,
    incident_date: str,
    duration_minutes: int,
    events: List[IncidentEvent],
    **kwargs,
) -> str:
    h = duration_minutes // 60
    m = duration_minutes % 60
    timeline = generate_timeline_table(events)
    impact = generate_impact_table(
        kwargs.get("users_affected", 0),
        kwargs.get("error_budget_pct", 0.0),
        kwargs.get("revenue_impact_usd", 0.0),
        kwargs.get("external_customers", 0),
        kwargs.get("tool_calls", 0),
        kwargs.get("irreversible_calls", 0),
    )
    return f"""# Postmortem: [{incident_id}] {title}

**Status:** Draft
**Severity:** {severity}
**Incident Commander:** @{ic}
**Lead Engineer:** @{lead_eng}
**Date of Incident:** {incident_date}
**Duration:** {h}h {m}m

## Impact Summary
{impact}

## Timeline of Events (UTC)
{timeline}

## Root Cause
[IC to populate]

## Contributing Factors
1. Why? ->
2. Why? ->

## Action Items
| ID    | Action | Owner | Due Date | Priority |
|-------|--------|-------|----------|----------|
| AI-01 |        |       |          | P0       |
"""


if __name__ == "__main__":
    events = [
        ("2026-07-14T02:15:00Z", "Error rate spike above 5% threshold", "PagerDuty"),
        ("2026-07-14T02:18:00Z", "On-call engineer acknowledged alert", "@alice"),
        ("2026-07-14T02:22:00Z", "SEV-2 declared, war room opened", "@alice"),
        ("2026-07-14T02:50:00Z", "Feature flag disabled, rollback applied", "@alice"),
        ("2026-07-14T02:58:00Z", "Error rate nominal, incident resolved", "@alice"),
    ]
    doc = scaffold_postmortem(
        "INC-2026-0042", "Agent Recursive Tool Call Exhaustion",
        "SEV-2", "alice", "bob", "2026-07-14", 43, events,
        users_affected=1200, error_budget_pct=18.5,
        revenue_impact_usd=4200.0, external_customers=3,
        tool_calls=847, irreversible_calls=2,
    )
    print(doc[:400])
Action Item Quality and Ownership

The action items section is the most operationally important part of a postmortem. A postmortem with vague action items like 'improve monitoring' or 'review the process' produces no measurable change. Each action item must have a single named owner (not a team), a concrete deliverable, a due date, and a priority. Priority should be P0 (this week), P1 (this sprint), or P2 (this quarter).

P0 action items should go into the active sprint immediately after the postmortem meeting. They should not wait for the next planning cycle. The IC is responsible for creating tickets for all P0 items before the postmortem meeting ends. P1 and P2 items go into the team backlog and are tracked in weekly incident review rituals.

For agentic AI systems, action items frequently fall into three categories: prompt hardening (adding constraints, stop conditions, or guardrails to system prompts), eval coverage (adding regression tests that catch the specific failure mode), and runtime guardrails (circuit breakers, tool call budgets, rate limits implemented in the agent framework). Each category requires a different team — prompt owners, evaluation engineers, and platform engineers respectively.
Postmortem Storage and Discoverability

Postmortems only provide institutional value if future teams can find and learn from them. Store postmortems in a searchable knowledge base with a consistent naming convention. Tag each postmortem with the affected service, the incident type (e.g., prompt-regression, tool-call-budget, eval-drift, infrastructure), and the severity. This tagging enables teams to query for all past incidents of a given type before shipping a new feature.

Monthly postmortem review sessions, where the SRE lead walks through recent postmortems with the broader engineering team, are one of the highest-leverage reliability practices available. They spread institutional knowledge about failure modes to engineers who were not part of the incident, and they surface patterns across incidents that any single team's retrospective would miss.
Key Takeaways
Initiate postmortems within 24 hours of resolution and hold the review meeting within 72 hours; memory degrades rapidly for non-deterministic AI incidents.
The canonical template has eight sections: impact summary, timeline, root cause, contributing factors, what went well/wrong/lucky, action items, and lessons learned.
AI-specific postmortems must include the system prompt snapshot, tool call sequence, model version, eval scores, and a replay link as appendix artifacts.
Blamelessness targets systems, not people — redirect questions from 'who' to 'what conditions made this failure possible'.
Every action item needs a single owner, a concrete deliverable, a due date, and a priority; P0 items go into the active sprint before the postmortem meeting ends.
